segments.title: French and Dutch ships in the Far Eastern balance
segments.summary: The scenario weighs French and Dutch East Indies cruisers, destroyers, submarines, and minesweepers as useful Allied additions rather than decisive fleets.
segments.body: The discussion begins by placing the French light cruiser Lamotte-Picquet and the Dutch East Indies Squadron into a wider Allied naval balance. De Ruyter, Tromp, Java, destroyers, submarines, mine forces, and minesweepers offer local knowledge and useful combat power, even if the speakers are cautious about exact availability. Tromp's small displacement also prompts a useful classification point: a ship described as a light cruiser could resemble a later large destroyer or flotilla leader in size and employment. The speakers explicitly acknowledge that they are extrapolating some Dutch availability backward from 1941, so the detailed order of battle is a scenario assumption rather than a firm historical claim. This watch point shows why a Far Eastern war would involve several colonial navies, not only Britain and Japan.

evidence.note: The speakers discuss Lamotte-Picquet, Dutch East Indies ships, and the prospective contribution of Dutch cruisers, destroyers, submarines, and minesweepers.

segments.title: Jutland's legacy for Royal Navy night fighting
segments.summary: The speakers argue that Royal Navy night-fighting practice, sharpened by Jutland, could offset Japan's later reputation for fighting after dark.
segments.body: In this counterfactual, avoiding detection matters as much as firepower, and the speakers connect that problem to British night-fighting preparation. They argue that the Royal Navy had spent two decades trying to avoid a repeat of the German escape at Jutland, leaving it well practiced by 1939. Training is presented here as institutional memory: frustration over a missed opportunity becomes a long-running programme of exercises and doctrine. The point challenges the assumption that Japanese night-combat skill was automatically unrivalled at the outbreak of war. Opening the video here gives the doctrinal reason behind the scenario's British confidence rather than merely asserting a tactical advantage.

evidence.note: The conversation links British night-fighting practice to the unresolved night action at Jutland.

segments.title: Japan's incomplete 1939 carrier force
segments.summary: A January 1939 war catches Japan before the carrier force, aircraft, and operational readiness associated with the Pacific War are fully in place.
segments.body: The speakers contrast the Japanese carrier force available in the scenario with the larger pool of British, American, and French carrier assets. Japan has lost Akagi in the scenario, Ryujo carries a comparatively small air group, and the concentrated Kido Butai familiar from 1941 does not yet exist. British options range from Eagle, Hermes, Argus, Glorious, Courageous, Furious, and the newly commissioned Ark Royal to Illustrious-class ships approaching completion. Pegasus is also mentioned as a seaplane carrier and aircraft transport, a role compared with the later support carrier HMS Unicorn. The speakers stress that later Japanese advantages should not be projected backward automatically because newer ships and aircraft were still incomplete, unlaunched, or not operational. This timing is central to the video's case that an early conflict produces a very different naval balance.

evidence.note: The speakers compare Japanese carriers and aircraft still coming into service with available Allied carriers.

segments.title: How a Pacific-first war changes British procurement
segments.summary: Without an immediate Atlantic convoy emergency, the scenario frees Britain to accelerate major warships and aircraft instead of concentrating on escorts.
segments.body: The discussion treats the Battle of the Atlantic as a powerful constraint on Britain's real wartime production choices. In the historical war, the immediate convoy emergency pushed the Royal Navy toward escorts and destroyers while major capital-ship programmes lost priority. Remove that Atlantic demand and battleships, carriers, and naval aircraft no longer compete with convoy protection in quite the same way. The speakers therefore expect construction already under way to accelerate rather than imagining an entirely new fleet appearing overnight. The watch point shows how war geography, industrial mobilisation, and urgent operational losses shape force structure long before fleets meet in battle.

evidence.note: The speakers contrast wartime escort production with the possibility of accelerating major units and aircraft in this scenario.

segments.title: The 1939 air balance before Japan's later fighters
segments.summary: The air comparison emphasizes A5M, A4N, and Ki-10 fighters rather than the later Japanese aircraft most familiar from 1941.
segments.body: The speakers argue that Hurricanes, P-36 Hawks, Gladiators, Sea Gladiators, and other available Allied fighters would face a much less formidable Japanese air arm in 1939. Their comparison centres on the Mitsubishi A5M, Nakajima A4N, and the army's biplane Kawasaki Ki-10 rather than the Zero and other aircraft associated with 1941. They judge the Hurricane clearly superior to the A5M while treating the Gladiator as less dominant but still competitive. Japan's later aircraft are described as the product of two more years of development outside the disruption of a wider war. The speakers also note that war can accelerate research while simultaneously forcing services to keep producing immediately available designs. This watch point separates the actual 1939 balance from hindsight shaped by the Pacific War.

evidence.note: The conversation compares the A5M, A4N, and Ki-10 with contemporary British, French, and American fighters.

segments.title: Japanese refits and an unready battle line
segments.summary: Japan's 1939 battle fleet has no Yamato-class ships and several important units caught in refit, limiting a rapid response to war.
segments.body: The discussion counts Japan's ten available Nagato-, Ise-, Fuso-, and Kongo-class battleships while emphasizing that no Yamato-class unit is ready. It then highlights ships whose refits remove them from the immediate balance. Hiei is described as stripped down in a reconstruction that cannot be reversed quickly, while Aoba- and Takao-class cruisers are also caught in lengthy work. Nominal fleet lists therefore exaggerate the combat power Japan could place at sea on short notice. The comparison is made against a Royal Navy that had repeatedly tested reserve mobilisation during the Abyssinia crisis, the Spanish Civil War, and later pre-war alarms. Those rehearsals give the British a practical readiness advantage in the scenario even before individual ship quality is compared.

evidence.note: The speakers discuss Japanese battleships, carriers, heavy cruisers in refit, and the relative readiness of British reserves.

segments.title: Shifting the Mediterranean Fleet east
segments.summary: Barham, Malaya, Warspite, and battleships nearing the end of refit give Britain options for moving the Mediterranean Fleet east while allies cover Europe.
segments.body: Barham, Malaya, and Warspite form the immediately useful core of the Mediterranean Fleet's eastward reinforcement. Ramillies is due out of refit, while Queen Elizabeth and Valiant are discussed as ships whose completion dates could change when the historical European war no longer interrupts the work. The speakers compare that improving British position with German and Italian World War I-era threats closer to home. French naval strength in the Mediterranean makes it more plausible for Britain to release major units without abandoning Europe. The scenario therefore treats redeployment as a balance among readiness, refit timing, allied cover, and deterrence rather than as a simple order to sail every battleship east.

evidence.note: The speakers identify Mediterranean Fleet battleships, ships emerging from refit, and French cover that could support an eastern deployment.

segments.title: Worked-up destroyer flotillas and forward reserves
segments.summary: Four experienced destroyer flotillas and Mediterranean reserve infrastructure make the proposed fleet transfer an organised force rather than a collection of capital ships.
segments.body: The speakers inventory John Cunningham's First Cruiser Squadron, including Arethusa and Penelope, alongside four destroyer flotillas already serving with the Mediterranean Fleet. Tribal-, G-, H-, and I-class ships had been commissioned and worked up through exercises, so the force brings trained formations rather than newly assembled escorts. They expect Britain to activate an older reserve flotilla to cover Mediterranean duties while French destroyers remain available. Malta, Alexandria, and Gibraltar also hold local destroyers, minesweepers, anti-submarine trawlers, and depot ships. This logistical depth matters because a fleet moving thousands of miles needs screening, maintenance, mine clearance, and base support as much as battleships. Opening the video here gives the supporting-force detail behind the scenario's claim that the Singapore strategy could function as designed.

evidence.note: The speakers list Mediterranean cruisers, Tribal-, G-, H-, and I-class destroyer flotillas, their exercise experience, and forward reserves at Malta, Alexandria, and Gibraltar.

segments.title: HMS Bulldog and the escort culture around Glorious
segments.summary: Glorious and her regular escort Bulldog illustrate both the practical screening relationship and the protective culture destroyer crews developed around capital ships.
segments.body: HMS Glorious adds another carrier to the eastward force, and the speakers note that HMS Bulldog regularly accompanied her. They describe destroyer crews as fiercely protective of the capital ships they screened, even while joking about them and occasionally suffering in collisions with much larger vessels. The anecdote turns an order-of-battle discussion into an explanation of fleet culture and mutual dependence. A carrier needs close escorts for navigation, anti-submarine work, rescue, and protection, while the destroyer accepts disproportionate physical risk beside the larger ship. The passage is also careful to present Bulldog's presence at Glorious's eventual sinking as an uncertain recollection rather than a settled claim.

evidence.note: The speakers discuss Glorious, Bulldog's attendant role, destroyer crews' protectiveness toward capital ships, and the physical risks of close fleet service.

segments.title: Could newly commissioned Ark Royal be sent east?
segments.summary: The speakers judge that Ark Royal's recent commissioning makes direct assignment to the Far East plausible before she becomes embedded in Home Fleet routines.
segments.question: Would the newly commissioned HMS Ark Royal be sent east with the reinforced fleet?
segments.answerShort: Probably. Because Ark Royal had only just commissioned and was not yet deeply integrated into the Home Fleet, the speakers see direct eastward assignment as plausible.
segments.body: The question tests whether a nominally available carrier could actually be detached from home commitments. Ark Royal's recent commissioning cuts both ways: she still needs work-up, but she is not yet central to an established Home Fleet deployment pattern. The speakers therefore lean toward sending her across to strengthen the carrier concentration. Their answer is a scenario judgement rather than a documented historical plan. It usefully shows that readiness includes organisational attachment and training state, not simply whether a ship appears on a fleet list.

evidence.note: One speaker asks where the newly commissioned Ark Royal would go, and the response argues that she could be sent directly east because she was not yet central to the Home Fleet setup.

segments.title: HMS Terror and the defence of Singapore
segments.summary: HMS Terror's 15-inch guns and local ammunition stocks strengthen Singapore as both a naval base and a defended heavy-artillery position.
segments.body: The monitor HMS Terror is treated as more than an isolated gun platform because her twin 15-inch guns are designed for heavy shore bombardment. Her presence gives Singapore two additional heavy guns alongside the fortress batteries. Just as importantly, stationing Terror there creates a reason to retain large stocks of 15-inch high-explosive ammunition in theatre. Those stocks could support both the monitor and the shore defences instead of being shipped away when a mobile fleet leaves. The watch point connects a single specialist warship to ammunition policy, harbour defence, and the wider Singapore strategy.

evidence.note: The discussion considers Terror's twin 15-inch armament, the resulting ammunition stocks, and the monitor's contribution to Singapore's heavy defences.

segments.title: Defending Singapore from the landward approach
segments.summary: The scenario treats Singapore as a naval base whose decisive defences must face inland, supported by earlier troop movement from India and Burma.
segments.body: The speakers compare Singapore with Portsmouth and Fort Nelson to make a basic point about naval-base defence: an attacker may land elsewhere and approach overland. They argue that Singapore therefore needs serious landward fortifications and capable commanders, not only guns pointed toward the sea. In this January 1939 scenario, Britain can move troops from India through Burma before Japan has the southern bases and simultaneous-operation capacity available in 1941. French Indochina is also not in Japanese hands, increasing the distance from which an assault must be organised. The result is a stronger defensive timetable in which geography, command quality, and troop transport reinforce the naval base.

evidence.note: The speakers compare Singapore's landward vulnerability with Portsmouth, then discuss earlier reinforcement from India and Burma and Japan's lack of southern staging bases.

segments.title: Matching fleet command to theatre logistics
segments.summary: The command discussion separates fleet leadership from theatre coordination, assigning different value to Dudley Pound, Percy Noble, and a moving Mediterranean Fleet.
segments.body: The speakers use the proposed redeployment to ask who should command ships at sea and who should coordinate a sprawling Far Eastern theatre. Dudley Pound would probably accompany most of his Mediterranean Fleet as the operational fleet commander. Percy Noble, already trusted and experienced as a cruiser commander on the China Station, is presented as the theatre coordinator. Pound's organisational strengths and Noble's local responsibilities are therefore separated rather than forcing one admiral to direct every base, route, reinforcement, and battle formation. The exchange illustrates how large naval operations require both command at sea and administrative control of the wider theatre.

evidence.note: The speakers discuss Dudley Pound, Percy Noble, fleet leadership, and the distinction between operational command and theatre coordination.

segments.title: Concentrating British carriers in the Far East
segments.summary: The Royal Navy could seek early superiority by concentrating Eagle, Hermes, Courageous, and Glorious while retaining Furious for European cover.
segments.body: The speakers move from command arrangements to the strength of the carrier force Britain could assemble against Japan in early 1939. They propose leaving Furious nearer Europe while sending Eagle, Hermes, Courageous, and Glorious east, with Argus potentially supporting the Indian Ocean. This concentration matters because Japan has not yet assembled the later Kido Butai and several British decks are already available. The proposed deployment still preserves a carrier for European deterrence rather than stripping every other theatre. Opening the video here shows how carrier numbers, readiness, and competing commitments shape the scenario before aircraft quality is considered.

evidence.note: The speakers argue that British reinforcements could achieve parity or superiority and list carriers available for the Far East and European cover.

segments.title: Moving fighters and bombers to Singapore
segments.summary: Carrier and merchant shipping can move Hurricanes and other aircraft east, while Wellingtons offer a more realistic long-range bomber contribution.
segments.body: The discussion treats aircraft movement as a maritime logistics problem because fighters that cannot fly the route must travel aboard Pegasus, Argus, or merchant ships. Hurricanes would strengthen Singapore's local air defence once unloaded and assembled. Wellingtons could instead self-deploy through friendly staging bases and operate at useful regional range. The bombers cannot reach Japan from Singapore, but they could help defend Hong Kong and attack forces moving against regional objectives. This keeps the scenario grounded in shipping capacity, staging bases, and aircraft range rather than simply assigning every available aircraft to the Far East.

evidence.note: The speakers discuss using carriers or cargo ships for fighters and the range and regional role of Wellingtons.

segments.title: A Pacific war redirects long-range bomber development
segments.summary: A war centred on East Asia would push the RAF toward longer range and different fuel-versus-bomb-load choices than its European bomber programme.
segments.body: The speakers point out that Britain's 1939 bomber force was designed around European distances. A conflict focused on Japan would create immediate pressure for much longer-ranged aircraft. They imagine a crash development path that might still produce a Lancaster, but one altered by different operational requirements. Extra fuel would displace bomb load, making range, payload, and basing the central design tradeoffs. This is explicitly counterfactual procurement analysis, not a claim that such an RAF programme already existed in January 1939.

evidence.note: The speakers describe the RAF bomber force as Eurocentric and predict pressure for longer range, a different Lancaster development path, and bomb-load-for-fuel tradeoffs.

segments.title: A Wellington and Swordfish combined attack
segments.summary: The scenario imagines Wellingtons drawing anti-aircraft fire and helping guide low-flying Swordfish in a coordinated attack on a Japanese force.
segments.body: The speakers acknowledge the low probability of level bombers hitting manoeuvring warships, but argue that bombers can still complicate an enemy's decisions. In their proposed sequence, Wellingtons overhead draw attention and anti-aircraft fire upward. Low-flying Swordfish then approach with torpedoes while the bombers use radio to guide them toward a force that lacks radar warning. The bombers act as distraction and target-location support rather than as the sole ship-killing weapon. The example highlights practical joint coordination and a layered attack instead of claiming that heavy bombers alone will sink a fleet.

evidence.note: The conversation describes bomber distraction, radio coordination, and a low-level Swordfish torpedo attack.

segments.title: Budget rivalry versus tactical cooperation
segments.summary: The speakers distinguish fierce inter-service arguments over money and strategy from generally effective tactical cooperation in operations.
segments.body: The discussion uses the combined air attack to separate two kinds of inter-service relationship. The Royal Navy and RAF could clash sharply when budgets and competing visions of imperial defence were at stake. At the tactical level, however, the speakers judge cooperation and radio coordination to be more normal than failure. They also note that Arthur Harris is not yet directing Bomber Command and that a Pacific war would make a purely Europe-focused bomber strategy difficult to sustain. Even so, Britain would retain aircraft at home to deter a separate European war, so cooperation in Asia remains constrained by global commitments.

evidence.note: The speakers contrast strategic budget rivalry with tactical cooperation, discuss Bomber Command without Harris, and retain forces in Europe for deterrence.

segments.title: Why January 1939 favours the Allies
segments.summary: Commonwealth air forces, access to American aircraft, and Japan's incomplete readiness make January 1939 an unusually favourable Allied moment.
segments.body: The discussion sees Australia and New Zealand as having a direct stake in keeping Japanese forces north of the Dutch East Indies. Their air forces would seek additional aircraft quickly, whether from British or American suppliers. The speakers expect the United States to continue selling aircraft such as the P-36 Hawk even if it does not enter the war. Japan, meanwhile, has not yet reached the carrier, aircraft, and fleet concentration associated with 1941. The result is a scenario in which timing, industrial access, allied geography, and Commonwealth self-interest reinforce one another.

evidence.note: The speakers discuss Australian and New Zealand interest, American aircraft sales, the P-36 Hawk, and Japan's incomplete 1939 readiness.

segments.title: Japan's northern army commitments slow a southern response
segments.summary: Troops committed in northern China and toward the Soviet frontier cannot rapidly shift across poor communications to attack Hong Kong or the southern colonies.
segments.body: The speakers place a large portion of the Japanese Army in northern China during the winter, facing or preparing to face the Soviet Union. Long and difficult lines of communication make those formations slow to redeploy south. That delay reduces Japan's ability to mount a quick attack on Hong Kong while Britain moves reinforcements through India, the Middle East, and Singapore. The passage treats the army's geography as a naval factor because it changes which ports and bases each side can threaten before fleets concentrate. This is a scenario assumption grounded in the speakers' reading of January 1939 commitments, not a claim that a specific southern operation had already been ordered.

evidence.note: The speakers describe Japanese troops tied down in northern China and toward the Soviet frontier, with communications too slow for a rapid move against Hong Kong.

segments.title: A possible Allied second front in China
segments.summary: Rather than invade Japan immediately, the speakers consider an Anglo-led expedition to reinforce China and force Japan to fight on another front.
segments.body: Japan's army is described as tied down by the China war and northern commitments, making a quick attack on Hong Kong harder. The speakers reject an immediate invasion of Japan as needlessly ambitious. Their alternative is a sizeable British expedition into China, joined by French Foreign Legion troops and perhaps colonial Dutch units. Chinese Nationalist forces would provide an existing partner army, while British jungle-trained formations and armoured elements add modern equipment. The resulting second front depends on naval sea control because every coalition force, vehicle, and supply must first reach and then remain connected to China.

evidence.note: The speakers discuss Japanese commitments in China, a British expeditionary force, French Foreign Legion troops, and Chinese Nationalist cooperation.

segments.title: Sea, road, and river supply routes into China
segments.summary: Sea communications, an accelerated India-China road or railway link, and the Yangtze, Pearl, and Yellow rivers could sustain an Allied expedition inland.
segments.body: The proposed China campaign depends first on the Royal Navy maintaining a sea line of communication for troops and supplies. The speakers then suggest accelerating the developing road or railway connection from India into China. The Yangtze, Pearl, and Yellow rivers could carry ocean-going shipping far inland, while existing river gunboats provide a starting point for local control. That river access contrasts with the D-Day problem of sustaining an army across open beaches without immediately captured ports. Inadequate roads and patchy railways would still limit the final movement of supplies, so water transport reduces rather than removes the inland logistics problem.

evidence.note: The conversation links a Royal Navy sea line of communication and an India-China land route with the Yangtze, Pearl, and Yellow rivers, inland shipping, and river gunboats.

segments.title: Blockade before invasion
segments.summary: The British objective in the scenario is to isolate Japan with cruisers and submarines, forcing the Japanese navy to choose between trade protection and fleet action.
segments.body: The speakers frame the Royal Navy's aim as strangulation rather than an immediate assault on the Japanese home islands. Town-class cruisers and submarines would attack or interrupt trade routes while the main Allied fleet formed farther south. Japan then has to decide whether to disperse forces to hunt surface raiders and protect merchants or concentrate for battle against the Royal Navy. Waiting also allows British reserve and Home Fleet units to join the Mediterranean reinforcements at Singapore. The dilemma turns Japanese operational choices, trade dependence, and the timing of fleet concentration into central parts of the counterfactual rather than assuming that one decisive battle is inevitable.

evidence.note: The speakers discuss cruiser and submarine pressure on Japanese trade and the choice between protecting commerce and seeking fleet action.

segments.title: Would France send a battleship east?
segments.summary: At least one French battleship might join the Allied force, although France's larger value may be covering British withdrawals in the Mediterranean.
segments.question: Could France be persuaded to send one of its newer battleships to the Far East?
segments.answerShort: Possibly. The speakers expect at least one French battleship could accompany the force, but French ships may instead be more valuable covering the Mediterranean while British units move east.
segments.body: The question asks how coalition strength should be divided between the Far East and Europe. One option is to add a modern French battleship directly to the Allied battle line. The alternative is indirect support, with French capital ships covering Mediterranean commitments vacated by the Royal Navy. The speakers lean toward at least one French battleship accompanying the fleet but do not treat that as certain. Their answer shows how an ally can enable a deployment either by contributing to the expedition or by holding another theatre.

evidence.note: One speaker asks whether France would send a newer battleship, and the reply weighs direct participation against French coverage of Britain's Mediterranean absence.

segments.title: The concentrated fleet battle the Royal Navy planned for
segments.summary: A combined Allied force of carriers, battleships, cruisers, and destroyer flotillas resembles the concentrated fleet action Royal Navy doctrine expected.
segments.body: The speakers imagine British units joined by Dutch and French ships, creating a force of roughly nine or ten battleships, several carriers, cruisers, and as many as six destroyer flotillas. Tribal-class formations and a senior destroyer commander give the screen the organisation expected for a major fleet battle. This is unusually favourable for the Royal Navy because its trade routes lie behind the battle area and Japan is one of the few opponents likely to seek a comparable concentration. Jutland remains the benchmark for formation, destroyer employment, command, and signals, with the joke about Beatty's signal lieutenants standing for lessons learned from 1916. The watch point shows the Royal Navy finally fighting the concentrated battle for which its interwar doctrine and exercises had prepared.

evidence.note: The speakers outline an Allied fleet concentration and compare its organisation and conditions to the fleet action the Royal Navy expected after Jutland.

segments.title: Night operations, radio beacons, and Swordfish
segments.summary: The proposed British advantage combines night-fighting experience, night flying, radio aids, and large numbers of Swordfish against an enemy without radar.
segments.body: The speakers do not assume that radar has already transformed the fleet, but they do point to British experience in night operations and radio beacons. Royal Navy aviators have practised night flying and can use those aids to recover and coordinate aircraft in darkness. Large numbers of Swordfish matter because their torpedo attacks can exploit that experience against a Japanese force still using earlier-generation aircraft and detection methods. The absence of Japanese radar in the scenario makes warning and interception more difficult. This is a tactical complement to the larger argument about fleet readiness and concentration rather than a claim of automatic British victory.

evidence.note: The speakers discuss night fighting, night flying, radio beacons, Swordfish, and the absence of Japanese radar.

segments.title: Submarine ambushes before the fleet battle
segments.summary: Forward-positioned British submarines and destroyer anti-submarine work could make the South China Sea or waters near Taiwan a dangerous approach route for Japan.
segments.body: The speakers expect Japan to come forward toward a prospective battle area in the South or East China Sea, perhaps around Taiwan rather than through the narrow strait. British submarines can wait on likely approach routes because the direction of the Japanese sortie is comparatively predictable. Numerous destroyers use ASDIC to protect the Allied main force from Japanese boats at the same time. T-class submarines are described as ambush predators designed to fire large salvoes at an enemy battle fleet, not merely as commerce raiders. A successful attack near the Japanese coast or fleet exit routes could damage capital ships before the surface fleets meet. The passage therefore makes the approach to battle, intelligence about routes, and anti-submarine screening as important as the battle itself.

evidence.note: The speakers consider likely battle waters, forward British submarines, destroyer ASDIC work, and the T-class anti-fleet role.

segments.title: Prize-taking and the missing Axis pact
segments.summary: British cruisers might capture Japanese merchantmen rather than sink them, while Germany and Italy are not yet bound to enter the conflict for Japan.
segments.body: The speakers distinguish British commerce warfare from indiscriminate sinking: cruisers would intercept Japanese merchantmen, place prize crews aboard, and send usable ships home. This makes blockade enforcement a way to enlarge Allied shipping while denying Japan cargo capacity. The scenario also occurs before Germany, Italy, and Japan are joined by the Tripartite Pact. No treaty therefore obliges Germany or Italy to enter the conflict for Japan. They might instead prefer Britain and France to remain distracted in the Far East, showing how the timing of alliances can be as important as fleet strength.

evidence.note: The speakers describe cruisers capturing Japanese merchant ships and note that no pact yet obliges Germany or Italy to support Japan.
